title: Get Data from Query Results Instead of Extra Lookup
description: When data is already available in the query results, use it directly instead of making additional lookups

before.code:
# UNOPTIMIZED: Extra lookup for data already in query
def execute(filters):
    # item_list already contains item details from JOIN
    item_list = get_items(filters)  # SELECT ... FROM Invoice JOIN Item
    
    # PROBLEM: Fetching ALL item details again separately
    item_details = get_item_details()  # SELECT * FROM Item
    
    for d in item_list:
        # Looking up item_record when d already has the data!
        item_record = item_details.get(d.item_code)
        
        row = {
            "item_code": d.item_code,
            # Using looked-up values instead of query result
            "item_name": item_record.item_name if item_record else d.item_name,
            "item_group": item_record.item_group if item_record else d.item_group,
            # ...
        }

after.code:
# OPTIMIZED: Use data from existing query results
def execute(filters):
    # item_list already contains item details from JOIN
    item_list = get_items(filters)  # SELECT ... FROM Invoice JOIN Item
    
    # FIX: Don't fetch item_details separately!
    # Removed: item_details = get_item_details()
    
    for d in item_list:
        # Use prefixed column names from JOIN result
        row = {
            "item_code": d.item_code,
            # Use joined data with aliased column names
            "item_name": d.si_item_name if d.si_item_name else d.i_item_name,
            "item_group": d.si_item_group if d.si_item_group else d.i_item_group,
            # ...
        }

energy_impact: Eliminates an entire redundant database query. If item table has 10000 rows, that's a significant query saved.
key_insight: When using JOINs, include all needed columns in the SELECT. Use column aliases to distinguish source tables. Avoid separate lookups.
